source_title: Bill Gurley: 6 Out of 10 People Are Making This Mistake

paragraphs.speaker: sam
paragraphs.name: Sam Parr
paragraphs.text: One of the craziest stats early in the book—you ran this survey and it said six or seven out of 10 people hate their job.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: We came across this Gallup poll that said 53% of people aren't engaged at work. So we asked like 1,000 people, if you could start your career over again, would you do it differently? And seven out of 10 said yes. We took that to Wharton People Analytics to do the official academic version. Their number came back six out of 10.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: There's a great book by Daniel Pink called The Power of Regrets. As you get older, your regrets of inaction start to really weigh on you. A regret of inaction is actively not doing something versus a regret of action is I made a mistake. The path I never traveled, the door I never opened is heavier in their mind.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: After the engineering degree, I went to Wall Street for three years as a sell-side research analyst. In both cases, I had a moment of reflection. I asked, do I see myself doing this 30 years from now? And in both cases, after about two or three years, I got to a no, even though I was doing well in those jobs.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: There's another exercise that Bezos is famous for—what he calls the regret minimization framework. He said, what would my 80 year old self advise me to do in this situation? Your 80 year old self is probably more risk seeking than you are in that moment, because he's trying to minimize that regret.

paragraphs.speaker: sam
paragraphs.name: Sam Parr
paragraphs.text: When I was 21 years old, I googled where do internet companies live? It said San Francisco. I cold emailed this guy named Brian, the CEO of Airbed and Breakfast. I emailed another employee named Justin and said I'm going to be there on Monday. Can I interview? I flew out there the next day, got the interview, got the job, went back to Tennessee, dropped out of college, sold everything I had.

paragraphs.speaker: sam
paragraphs.name: Sam Parr
paragraphs.text: My parents are entrepreneurs in Missouri. They started their entrepreneurial career owning a fruit stand. I told them I'm gonna leave school, I'm gonna join this thing. And they're like, okay, cool. We'll come pack up your stuff. That emotional support is one of the biggest things that separates what allows people to win versus allow people to lose.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: Even though this book is written towards the person on the career path, I think it will be particularly interesting to parents and advisors to test whether or not they're willing to help support people in a way that your parents did.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: In the chapter titled Chase Your Curiosity, I tried to borrow as many of those type of exercises as possible. The very hardest part is to figure out actually what it is that drives you in this way. That's going to make it very easy for you to differentiate yourself.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: There's a story in the book about this guy, Bert Beveridge in Austin, Texas, who's 40 years old and he's watching a PBS show. They say to take a sheet of paper, draw a line down the middle, write on the left what you love to do and on the right what you're good at and try and find something in the middle. Through that exercise, he quit being a mortgage broker and launched a vodka company called Tito's at the age of 40.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: I once asked Jeff Bezos how he could possibly be good at angel investing while he's running this company. He did Google, Uber—it's a murderer's road. He was in the seed round of Google. He said the only thing he looks for is this insane determinism. He wanted to believe the person was going to go do this no matter what come hell or high water.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: We highlight in the book that Angela Duckworth found this podcast she had done 10 years later where she said it probably shouldn't have been 50-50, that it needed to put more weight on passion. She believes that we've taught children, young adults how to grind. We've taught them to persevere for the sake of perseverance. And she believes that now that leads to burnout.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: For people that are in the right space, honing your craft comes free. You just do it naturally. You wake up and want to do it. You're drawn to knowing more about the craft. I think it's hard to do all the things I recommend in this book if you don't truly love it.

paragraphs.speaker: bill
paragraphs.name: Bill Gurley
paragraphs.text: We titled the chapter chase your curiosity. What is it that you can't ignore?
